intro:
Initially I think I'm in bed, and this fact is confusing. No matter what black-out blinds or reinforced curtains we've put up in the window, some street light always gets through. But this room is black. 

I then notice that I can't move. I can feel some kind of frame keeping my head pointing in one direction, and my legs and hands are held fast. I'm not horizontal either - I'm strapped to something, held up and bound to a board. My arms are bent at the elbow, and I move my fingertips. I can feel some kind of keyboard underneath them. 

It is at this point that I begin to panic. I pull my body one way and the other and notice - whatever I'm attached to isn't rigid. I can feel it move. Without thinking I shout "help! help!". No response. I smash the keys under my fingertips. There is a loud noise, like a breaker switch being flipped. My eyes are dazzled by a bright light.

help.default:
Cross Words is a game based around cryptic crosswords. You won't need to solve every cryptic clue in the game in order to progress, but you will need to solve some. Guidance on solving cryptic clues can be found with HELP CRYPTIC. Cross words is also a tricky game. Most of what you read can be a part of one puzzle or another. Here are some clear, unambiguous rules: No text in help dialogues will ever be a part of a puzzle. No text which you speak in the game will ever be a part of a puzzle. Beyond this, you will have to rely on your wits.

The game uses a set of standard Interactive Fiction verbs. If you are new to Interactive Fiction, you can see a list of these verbs with HELP VERBS. This list is not exhaustive. If you are stuck, you may be given some items to help you with certain clues in the game. If you remain stuck, there is a hint system in the game. This is by default turned off. Type SETTINGS to access settings, and SETTINGS HINTS to toggle hints. Type HELP HINTS to see how to use the hints in game.

If you want to know more about the creator of Cross Words, then type HELP CREDITS.

Thanks for playing!

topics.credits:
Cross Words was written by A. James Sigston in Mar/Apr 2026. There exists a separate Lua and TypeScript implementation. The TypeScript implementation can be played at https://a-james.com/cross-words, and the Lua engine source code can be found at github.com/Sigston/diversion. The game data files have not been released, just like crossword setters never reveal their solutions!

The source code was largely written by Claude Code following architecture and engineering instructions from A. James Sigston. A human (AJS) also checked and corrected most of the code. Every word you see in the game was written by A. James Sigston, all puzzles were authored solely by A. James Sigston, and all creative decisions were made by A. James Sigston. Claude Code was used for data entry tasks, as coding up all the game logic, puzzles and text in JSON files was laborious. There is an AI Policy on the GitHub site above.
topics.cryptic:
If you have never solved a cryptic crossword before, they may seem very strange. They have much more in common with riddles than with non-cryptic crossword clues. The biggest barrier to getting good at cryptics is understanding what you should be looking for. You will learn common patterns and component parts as you play, but it is vital at first to understand what exactly a cryptic clue expects of you.

It is useful to see each word as an ingredient in an equation - no word is ever there purely for colour. Every word counts. Usually there is some hinge which splits the clue in two ("is" is a typical hinge), with one part representing the "defintion" and the other the "clue". The definition will be a synonym for the answer, whereas the clue will contain more puzzle elements. Working out where the definition is (and this must always be at the very beginning or the very end) can be a quick way to crack a clue.

What will the puzzle elements be in the clue part? This is the great unknown, and part of the fun of solving cryptic clues, but there are a series of common patterns. You should be thinking about whether what you see fits one pattern or another. Taking the whole thing as an equation, the simplest form of clue is a list of words which are to be identified and placed one after another. FORMER LOVER CAN COURT NO MORE breaks down into FORMER LOVER CAN COURT and NO MORE, with NO MORE as the definition. FORMER LOVER = EX, CAN = TIN and COURT = CT. These add up to EXTINCT, which means NO MORE. Here you can see the ways in which words can be exchanged - EX is a straight synonym for FORMER LOVER; CT is a common shortening for COURT; and a TIN is a kind of CAN. These substitutions are not arbitrary, and you cannot shorten anything into anything else. A reference dictionary usually provides the absolute say. Learning lots of short synonyms, and getting a feel for what shortening are commonly used, goes a long way to being a good solver.

You should also be looking for: words which indicate the ordering of parts, i.e., "backs", "follows", etc.; words for reversing the order of things; words for anagramming things, i.e., "mixed up", "broken", etc.; and words referring you to parts of words, i.e., "first", "starts", "empty", "middle". Other puzzle patterns are: sounds like (usually with an indicator word, such as "talking", "sounds"); hidden words (usually with "in": a word will be in another word); double definition (where there are two definitions rather than a clue); and the often very difficult "all in one" clue. Here, the entire phrase is the definition.

For more information on solving cryptic crosswords, you should read Two Girls, One on Each Knee by Alan Connor, watch Cracking the Cryptic on YouTube, or visit https://www.indigoextra.com/how-to-solve-cryptic-crosswords, which has a few examples. The creator's favourite cryptic crossword publication is the Times, and many of the clues in this game are in that style.
topics.hints: Every clue in the game relates to an object in the world. If some writing is placed on a mirror, HINT MIRROR will provide hints about that writing, if it is in fact a clue, and if there is a mirror in the world. There are only hints about cryptic crossword type clues - there are no hints about other puzzles. Asking for a hint the first time will reveal what kind of wordplay is present in the clue. Asking for a hint a second time will reveal where the definition is. Asking for a hint a third time will provide an additional clue. Asking for more hints will just restate these three pieces of information.
topics.verbs:
This is a non-exhaustive list of the verbs used in Cross Words. This list is intended to acquaint a player new to Interactive Fiction with the standard roster of commands. Part of the fun of a new Interactive Fiction game is working out which verbs to use - some entirely new - and in what ways to use them.

	LOOK (L): Looking will return a description of what you can currently see.
	EXAMINE (X): Examining a object will return its description. You may glean information by examining something that isn't obvious when just LOOKing into the room.
	READ (R): Some objects can be examined, but also read. Typing READ and the object's name will you give you what can be read, or a summary of it.
	INVENTORY (I): You can hold items and carry them around with you. To see a list of what you're carrying, type INVENTORY.
